cases.title: Trump University fraud litigation ended in a $25 million settlement without an admission of wrongdoing
cases.status: Settlement affirmed; no admission of wrongdoing
cases.summary: After years of consumer-fraud litigation over Trump University, the parties reached a $25 million settlement: $21 million for the federal class actions and $4 million for the New York attorney general's case. The Ninth Circuit affirmed final approval in 2018. The agreement expressly did not require Trump or the other defendants to admit wrongdoing.
cases.significance: The settlement returned an estimated 80 to 90 percent of eligible students' payments and resolved a major case alleging that Trump's branded educational venture used deceptive sales practices. It is strong evidence of the scale and cost of the dispute, but not an adjudication that Trump personally committed fraud.

sources.label: Simpson v. Trump, Ninth Circuit opinion affirming settlement approval
sources.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

cases.title: A New York court found that Trump breached fiduciary duties in his charitable foundation
cases.status: Adjudicated civil fiduciary breach; foundation dissolved
cases.summary: The Donald J. Trump Foundation dissolved under court supervision in December 2018. In November 2019, a New York judge found that Donald Trump had breached fiduciary duties and ordered him to pay $2 million in damages, directing that the money go to charities. The foundation's remaining assets had already been distributed to approved charities.
cases.significance: This is an adjudicated example of Trump using a charitable entity without the basic separation and governance required of a fiduciary. The court record is more probative than generalized criticism because it identifies a legal duty, a breach, and a monetary remedy.

sources.label: Matter of People v. Trump, decision and order
sources.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

sources.label: Attorney General summary of court order, dissolution, and governance remedies
sources.publisher: Office of the New York Attorney General

cases.title: Civil judgments hold Trump liable for sexually abusing and repeatedly defaming E. Jean Carroll
cases.status: $5 million judgment affirmed, with certiorari denied and a rehearing petition pending; $83.3 million judgment affirmed with Supreme Court review not exhausted as of access
cases.summary: A federal jury found Trump civilly liable for sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll and defaming her, awarding $5 million. The Second Circuit affirmed. The Supreme Court denied certiorari on June 29, 2026, and Trump filed a petition for rehearing on July 8 that remained pending as of July 13. A second jury awarded $83.3 million for separate defamatory statements; the Second Circuit affirmed that judgment in September 2025 and denied rehearing in April 2026. As of July 13, 2026, Trump had announced but had not yet exhausted Supreme Court review of the second judgment.
cases.significance: The judgments provide adjudicated civil findings concerning sexual misconduct and a sustained campaign of public defamation. They directly test attempts to dismiss the conduct as merely offensive rhetoric: juries found legally compensable injury, and appellate courts upheld the verdicts reviewed so far.

sources.label: Second Circuit opinion affirming the $5 million verdict

cases.title: New York appellate judges left Trump's civil-fraud liability intact but vacated the massive disgorgement award
cases.status: Liability and nonmonetary relief affirmed; disgorgement vacated; further review unresolved in sources reviewed
cases.summary: A New York trial court found persistent fraud in financial statements used in business transactions and entered broad relief, including hundreds of millions of dollars in disgorgement. On August 21, 2025, the Appellate Division affirmed the liability findings and nonmonetary relief but unanimously vacated the disgorgement awards as excessive under the Eighth Amendment. The sources reviewed do not establish a final resolution of further appellate proceedings.
cases.significance: The surviving liability findings document a repeated pattern of materially false asset valuations rather than a single verbal exaggeration. At the same time, the appellate reversal of the monetary award is essential context and prevents the site from presenting the original dollar judgment as still operative.

sources.label: Trial court decision and judgment in People v. Trump
sources.publisher: New York State Unified Court System

cases.title: A New York jury convicted Trump on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records
cases.status: Convicted and sentenced to unconditional discharge; direct appeal perfected and pending
cases.summary: On May 30, 2024, a Manhattan jury found Trump guilty on 34 counts of falsifying business records in the first degree. The court entered judgment and imposed an unconditional discharge on January 10, 2025, meaning no incarceration, fine, or probation. Court records show that Trump perfected his direct appeal in 2026; the conviction therefore remains under appellate review.
cases.significance: This is the only entry in this set based on a criminal jury conviction of Trump himself. The unusual sentence did not erase the guilty verdict, but the pending appeal means the site should not call the conviction final and unreviewable.

cases.title: The Mueller report documented obstruction evidence but made no prosecutorial judgment against a sitting president
cases.status: Investigative report; no obstruction charge and no adjudication
cases.summary: Special Counsel Robert Mueller's second report volume analyzed multiple episodes in which Trump sought to influence or constrain the Russia investigation. Mueller explained that the Justice Department's policy against indicting a sitting president shaped the office's approach: it did not make a traditional charging decision. Mueller also said that if investigators had confidence Trump clearly did not commit a crime, the report would have said so, and emphasized that the report did not exonerate him.
cases.significance: The record supplies detailed, evidence-based examples of presidential pressure on investigators and witnesses. Its force comes from the documented conduct, not from pretending Mueller issued a criminal verdict that he explicitly declined to make.

sources.label: Report on the Investigation into Russian Interference, Volume II
Special Counsel Mueller's May 29, 2019 statement

cases.title: The Government Accountability Office found that the Trump administration illegally withheld Ukraine security assistance
cases.status: Official GAO finding of an Impoundment Control Act violation
cases.summary: The Office of Management and Budget withheld approximately $214 million in congressionally appropriated Defense Department security assistance for Ukraine while the administration pursued a policy objective. On January 16, 2020, the nonpartisan Government Accountability Office concluded that OMB had violated the Impoundment Control Act because the executive branch cannot substitute its policy priorities for Congress's enacted funding decisions.
cases.significance: The finding provides a narrow, formal legal judgment about executive misuse of spending authority at the center of the Ukraine pressure campaign. It is independent of the political judgments reached in impeachment proceedings.

sources.label: GAO Decision B-331564, Office of Management and Budget—Withholding of Ukraine Security Assistance

cases.title: The House impeached Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress; the Senate acquitted him
cases.status: Impeached on two articles; acquitted by the Senate on both
cases.summary: The House of Representatives impeached Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress after finding that he used presidential authority to pressure Ukraine for investigations that would benefit him politically and then directed defiance of the House inquiry. On February 5, 2020, the Senate voted 48 guilty to 52 not guilty on abuse of power and 47 guilty to 53 not guilty on obstruction, resulting in acquittal on both articles.
cases.significance: The episode joins the Ukraine pressure campaign to a constitutional accountability process. The House judgment was grave and specific, but a complete account must include that the Senate did not reach the two-thirds threshold needed to convict and remove.

sources.label: H. Res. 755, Articles of Impeachment Against President Donald J. Trump

cases.title: The January 6 committee documented a multi-part effort to overturn the 2020 election
cases.status: Congressional factual findings and criminal referrals; not a court judgment
cases.summary: The House Select Committee's final report assembled testimony, documents, and public evidence describing a sustained effort led by Trump to reverse his election defeat. The report covered false fraud claims, pressure on state officials, a false-elector plan, efforts to enlist the Justice Department, pressure on Vice President Pence, the Ellipse speech, and Trump's failure for hours to stop the Capitol attack. The committee also issued nonbinding criminal referrals.
cases.significance: The report connects episodes that can look isolated into a coherent attempt to remain in power despite the certified result. It is a congressional investigation, however, and its referrals were not convictions or binding prosecutorial findings.

sources.label: Final Report of the Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
Select Committee criminal referrals and supporting material

cases.title: The federal election-subversion prosecution ended without trial after Trump's reelection
cases.status: Charges dismissed without prejudice; no trial or merits verdict
cases.summary: A federal grand jury indicted Trump over alleged conspiracies and obstruction aimed at overturning the 2020 election. After Trump won the 2024 election, Special Counsel Jack Smith moved to dismiss the case under the Justice Department's longstanding position that a sitting president cannot be federally prosecuted. The court dismissed without prejudice on November 25, 2024. Smith's later report said his office believed the admissible evidence would have been sufficient to convict, but that prosecutorial assessment was never tested at trial.
cases.significance: The case placed detailed election-subversion allegations in a criminal pleading, but reelection—not a jury verdict—terminated the prosecution. Its procedural ending is central to the accountability story and must not be misdescribed as either exoneration or conviction.

sources.label: Federal court record for United States v. Trump, No. 1:23-cr-257
Special Counsel Smith report, Volume One

cases.title: Georgia's election-interference prosecution was dismissed in full without a trial
cases.status: All remaining charges dismissed on the prosecutor's motion; no merits verdict
cases.summary: A Fulton County grand jury indicted Trump and 18 co-defendants in August 2023 on racketeering and related charges arising from efforts to change Georgia's 2020 election result. After District Attorney Fani Willis was disqualified and a replacement prosecutor reviewed the case, the state filed a nolle prosequi. On November 26, 2025, Judge Scott McAfee granted the motion and dismissed the case in its entirety. Trump was never tried on the allegations.
cases.significance: The indictment captured conduct including the call asking Georgia's secretary of state to find 11,780 votes, but institutional and procedural breakdowns prevented a merits trial. The dismissal is a major accountability failure or safeguard depending on perspective; either way, it cannot be presented as proof of guilt.

sources.label: State's motion to enter nolle prosequi and prosecutorial review
Order granting dismissal of the Georgia election case

cases.title: The House impeached Trump for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him despite a 57-vote guilty majority
cases.status: Impeached; acquitted because the Senate fell short of a two-thirds vote
cases.summary: One week after the Capitol attack, the House impeached Trump for incitement of insurrection, citing his months of false election claims, pressure on Georgia officials, and January 6 speech. On February 13, 2021, 57 senators voted guilty and 43 voted not guilty. That bipartisan majority fell short of the Constitution's two-thirds threshold, so the Senate acquitted him.
cases.significance: The second impeachment was the constitutional branch's most direct response to Trump's role in the attack on the transfer of power. Both facts matter: a majority of senators voted to convict, and the constitutionally required supermajority did not.

sources.label: H. Res. 24, article of impeachment for incitement of insurrection
Senate roll call on the second impeachment article

cases.title: Trump granted sweeping clemency to January 6 defendants on returning to office
cases.status: Completed lawful exercise of presidential clemency
cases.summary: On his first day back in office, Trump commuted the sentences of 14 named January 6 defendants to time served, granted full and unconditional pardons to all other people convicted of January 6-related offenses, and directed the Justice Department to seek dismissal with prejudice of pending indictments. The proclamation was categorical rather than individualized by offense or conduct.
cases.significance: The proclamation used a broad constitutional power to erase or halt federal consequences for the attack that disrupted certification of Trump's election defeat. It sharply reversed the Justice Department's years-long accountability effort and included people convicted of serious violence as well as lower-level offenses.

cases.title: The classified-documents indictment was dismissed on appointment grounds and never reached trial
cases.status: Indictment dismissed; appeal ended as to Trump after reelection; no merits verdict
cases.summary: Federal prosecutors charged Trump with willful retention of national-defense information, obstruction, concealment, and false-statement offenses related to documents kept after his presidency. On July 15, 2024, Judge Aileen Cannon dismissed the indictment after concluding that Special Counsel Jack Smith had been appointed in violation of the Appointments Clause. Following Trump's reelection, the Justice Department ended its appeal as to him under sitting-president policy. No jury decided the factual allegations.
cases.significance: The indictment described unusually sensitive records and alleged attempts to obstruct their recovery, but the prosecution failed on a threshold structural issue before trial. The distinction between disturbing allegations and an unadjudicated, dismissed case is indispensable to a credible evidence site.

sources.label: Superseding indictment in United States v. Trump, Nauta, and De Oliveira

cases.title: Congressional investigators documented millions in foreign-government payments to Trump businesses while he was president
cases.status: Payments documented; Emoluments Clause claims not finally adjudicated on the merits
cases.summary: A 2024 report from Democratic staff of the House Oversight Committee, based on accounting records produced by Trump's former firm Mazars, identified at least $7.8 million paid by at least 20 foreign governments or state-controlled entities to four Trump businesses during two years of his first term. Earlier constitutional lawsuits over alleged emoluments ended as moot after he left office, with judgments vacated rather than a final Supreme Court merits ruling.
cases.significance: Trump retained ownership of a global business while exercising presidential power, creating recurring channels through which foreign states could spend money at properties benefiting him. The documented receipts establish the conflict mechanism even though courts did not finally decide whether particular transactions violated the Constitution.

cases.title: The administration implemented family separation without adequate planning for children or reunification
cases.status: Inspector General failures documented; class-action settlement approved
cases.summary: The Trump administration's zero-tolerance prosecution policy led to the separation of more than 3,000 children from parents at the southwest border. A Justice Department Inspector General review found that senior leaders understood prosecutions would cause separations but failed to prepare for the consequences, coordinate with partner agencies, or plan reunification. The government later settled the Ms. L. class action, providing reunification and support measures and limiting future separations; the settlement did not award damages.
cases.significance: The record shows that harm to children was a foreseeable consequence of a deterrence-focused policy, not an unforeseeable administrative accident. The failures were documented by the administration's own watchdog and produced continuing court-supervised remedies.

sources.label: DOJ Inspector General report on planning and implementation of zero tolerance

cases.title: A federal judge found Trump's mass inspector-general removals violated statutory notice requirements
cases.status: Statutory violation found; reinstatement denied and case stayed pending higher-court guidance
cases.summary: On January 24, 2025, Trump removed more than a dozen inspectors general without giving Congress the advance notice and substantive, case-specific reasons required by the Inspector General Act. In September 2025, a federal district judge concluded that the removals violated the statute. The court nevertheless denied reinstatement and other permanent injunctive relief and stayed proceedings while related Supreme Court separation-of-powers litigation remained pending.
cases.significance: Inspectors general are an internal check on waste, fraud, abuse, and political retaliation across the executive branch. Removing a large group without legally required notice weakened that check and tested whether statutory accountability safeguards can produce an effective remedy.

sources.label: District court memorandum opinion in Storch v. Trump

cases.title: The Supreme Court held Trump's birthright-citizenship order unconstitutional
cases.status: Final Supreme Court merits decision affirming that the executive order is unconstitutional
cases.summary: Executive Order 14160 directed federal agencies to deny recognition of citizenship to certain children born in the United States based on their parents' immigration status. On June 30, 2026, the Supreme Court held that children born in the United States to parents who are unlawfully present or temporarily present are citizens at birth under the Fourteenth Amendment and federal law, and affirmed judgment against the order.
cases.significance: The order attempted by unilateral executive action to narrow a constitutional citizenship guarantee affecting children born on U.S. soil. The Supreme Court's merits ruling supplies a definitive example of presidential action exceeding constitutional limits.

sources.label: Executive Order 14160, Protecting the Meaning and Value of American Citizenship

cases.title: Trump pardoned political allies and associates convicted in cases connected to investigations of his circle
cases.status: Completed lawful exercise of presidential clemency
cases.summary: In the closing weeks of his first term, Trump pardoned former national security adviser Michael Flynn, longtime adviser Roger Stone, former campaign chairman Paul Manafort, and Charles Kushner, the father of Trump's son-in-law. The recipients' cases included false statements, obstruction, witness tampering, tax and bank fraud, conspiracy, and campaign-connected conduct. The pardons were constitutionally valid and ended or relieved federal punishment.
cases.significance: The pattern raises a conflict-of-interest question: a president used personal clemency power to benefit allies, a campaign chairman, an adviser who resisted an investigation, and a close relative of his family. That is a legitimate abuse-of-power critique even without claiming the pardons were illegal.

sources.label: Pardons granted by President Donald J. Trump, 2017-2021
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of the Pardon Attorney

cases.title: Trump amplified a video calling for democratic socialism to be criminalized and its leaders deported
cases.status: Documented presidential social-media amplification; the quoted demands were Michael Savage's, not Trump's own words
cases.summary: On July 12, 2026, Donald Trump's Truth Social account shared a YouTube link to Michael Savage's video titled "DEMOCRATIC SOCIALISM MUST BE CRIMINALIZED; LEADERS DEPORTED." Trump added no accompanying explanation or disclaimer. In the video, Savage argued for criminalizing democratic socialism and deporting its leaders while discussing New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders. The record establishes Trump's amplification of that material; it does not establish that Trump authored or adopted every statement in the video.
cases.significance: A sitting president's decision to distribute a call to criminalize a peaceful political ideology and deport its leaders conflicts with constitutional pluralism, even without treating every word as Trump's own. The First Amendment protects political speech and association. The narrow grievance is the use of presidential reach to amplify punishment of political belief—not a claim that Trump issued an arrest or deportation order.

sources.label: Trump's Truth Social post linking to the Savage video
Michael Savage video shared by Trump
Constitution Annotated overview of First Amendment freedom of political association
Reporting on Trump's share and the video's attributed statements

cases.title: The federal government accused Trump Management of systemic housing discrimination
cases.status: Consent decree; no admission of liability
cases.summary: The Justice Department sued Trump Management, Donald Trump, and Fred Trump under the Fair Housing Act, alleging that Black applicants were denied apartments or steered away while white applicants received offers. The case ended in a consent decree requiring nondiscrimination measures, fair-housing advertising, vacancy reporting, and other compliance steps. The decree expressly said it was not an admission by the defendants.
cases.significance: This early federal case is a documented starting point for evaluating the Trump business record on race. Its force comes from the filed allegations and binding remedial decree, while its no-admission clause must remain visible.

cases.title: Trump demanded harsher punishment during the Central Park Five case and maintained his position after exoneration
cases.status: Documented public campaign; convictions vacated
cases.summary: During the 1989 Central Park attack case, Trump bought full-page ads calling to reinstate the death penalty and demanding fear-inducing punishment of violent offenders. The five teenagers later had their convictions vacated after another man's confession was corroborated by DNA evidence, and New York City settled their civil case. Asked in 2019 whether he would apologize, Trump declined and said, misleadingly, that they had admitted guilt.
cases.significance: The episode shows the human cost of using celebrity and money to intensify a racially charged prosecution, followed by an unwillingness to reconsider after the legal record changed dramatically.

sources.label: Trump's May 1989 death-penalty advertisement
New York City comptroller statement approving the settlement
Trump's 2019 response declining to apologize

cases.title: Trump mainstreamed the false claim that Barack Obama was not born in the United States
cases.status: False claim contradicted by official records; later retracted
cases.summary: Trump spent years promoting doubt about President Barack Obama's U.S. birth even after Hawaii officials and the White House released the long-form birth certificate. In September 2016 he finally stated that Obama was born in the United States, while adding the unsupported falsehood that Hillary Clinton had started the controversy.
cases.significance: The campaign exploited a racist conspiracy theory to delegitimize the first Black president and demonstrated how repetition could move an evidence-free claim into mainstream political debate.

cases.title: The Access Hollywood recording captured Trump boasting about sexually aggressive conduct
cases.status: Authenticated recorded statement
cases.summary: A 2005 recording published in 2016 captured Trump using degrading language about women and describing kissing and grabbing women, framed as conduct celebrity allowed him to initiate. Trump apologized for the words while dismissing them as locker-room talk. The recording was later admitted as evidence in E. Jean Carroll's civil trial, and the Second Circuit upheld that evidentiary ruling.
cases.significance: The words are direct evidence of Trump's own attitude toward consent, entitlement, and women, rather than a secondhand characterization by an opponent.

sources.label: Original publication of the 2005 recording and transcript
Second Circuit opinion affirming admission of the recording in Carroll v. Trump
sources.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit via GovInfo

cases.title: Trump disparaged John McCain's military service because he was captured
cases.status: Documented recorded statement
cases.summary: At the 2015 Family Leadership Summit, Trump said of Navy veteran and former prisoner of war John McCain, 'He's not a war hero' and 'I like people that weren't captured,' before partially retreating under questioning. McCain endured more than five years of captivity and torture in North Vietnam.
cases.significance: The remark exemplifies contempt for sacrifice when the person involved is a political adversary, contradicting Trump's cultivated image of exceptional respect for service members and veterans.

Video and contemporaneous report of the McCain remarks

cases.title: Trump used a jerking pantomime while attacking disabled reporter Serge Kovaleski
cases.status: Recorded conduct; intent disputed by Trump
cases.summary: While defending his unsupported claim that thousands of Muslims in New Jersey celebrated the September 11 attacks, Trump imitated New York Times reporter Serge Kovaleski with flailing and bent-arm gestures. Kovaleski has arthrogryposis, which limits joint movement. Trump denied mocking the disability and said he was generically depicting a flustered reporter.
cases.significance: The episode joined an inflammatory false claim about Muslims with a degrading personal attack on a journalist whose reporting contradicted Trump.

cases.title: Trump assigned blame to 'both sides' after the white-supremacist rally in Charlottesville
cases.status: Documented official remarks; context preserved
cases.summary: After a white-supremacist and neo-Nazi rally in Charlottesville produced racist violence and the killing of Heather Heyer, Trump said there was 'blame on both sides' and 'very fine people on both sides.' In the same August 15 exchange, he explicitly said he was not referring to neo-Nazis and white nationalists and that they should be condemned totally.
cases.significance: Even with the explicit condemnation included, the remarks cast counterprotesters and participants in an event organized around white supremacy as morally comparable, providing rhetorical cover at a moment requiring clarity.

August 14 statement condemning racist groups

cases.title: Trump made false testing assurances and publicly speculated about injecting disinfectant during COVID-19
cases.status: Documented false assurance and unqualified medical speculation
cases.summary: At the CDC on March 6, 2020, Trump said that anybody who wanted a COVID-19 test could get one, while hospitals were reporting severe testing shortages and delays. At an April 23 briefing, after hearing a presentation on disinfectants' effects on surfaces, he asked whether disinfectant could be used 'by injection inside' or through another internal cleaning process and directed doctors to check it.
cases.significance: Presidential communication during an emergency can shape life-or-death choices. False reassurance obscured a major capacity failure, while improvising about dangerous substances at the podium displaced careful medical guidance.

sources.label: March 6 CDC visit transcript
April 23 coronavirus briefing transcript
National hospital pulse survey documenting testing and supply shortages
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Inspector General

cases.title: Trump pursued a multi-part campaign to overturn an election he had lost
cases.status: Congressional finding grounded in testimony and records
cases.summary: The House January 6 Committee found that Trump knowingly disseminated false fraud claims, pressured state officials and the Justice Department, supported a false-elector effort, pressured Vice President Mike Pence, and summoned supporters to Washington as part of an attempt to remain in power despite losing the 2020 election. State certifications and repeated court defeats contradicted the core fraud narrative.
cases.significance: The peaceful transfer of power is a constitutional baseline. The record describes not one angry statement but an organized effort across courts, states, federal agencies, Congress, and a mass gathering.

sources.label: Final Report of the Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack

cases.title: Trump's account reposted a racist video depicting Barack and Michelle Obama as apes, and he refused to apologize
cases.status: Documented social-media post and official response
cases.summary: Trump's Truth Social account reposted a false-election video that ended with Barack and Michelle Obama depicted as apes. The post was deleted after public condemnation. Trump said he had not watched the whole video, refused to apologize, said he had not made a mistake, and kept the staff member the White House blamed for posting it.
cases.significance: The ape trope has a long racist history when directed at Black people. A presidential account's publication of it, followed by refusal to apologize or discipline anyone, is evidence of both degrading conduct and institutional tolerance.

sources.label: Official transcript: deleted post portraying the Obamas as apes

cases.title: Two Trump Organization companies were convicted on all 17 tax-fraud and related felony counts
cases.status: Corporate criminal conviction
cases.summary: A Manhattan jury convicted two Trump Organization entities on all 17 felony counts, including tax fraud, falsifying business records, conspiracy, and a scheme to defraud, based on off-the-books executive compensation. The companies were sentenced to the statutory maximum fine of about $1.6 million. Donald Trump and his children were not personally charged in that prosecution.
cases.significance: The verdict establishes criminal wrongdoing inside the family company at the executive level. Accurate attribution is essential: the convicted defendants were corporate entities, not Donald Trump personally.

cases.title: Trump installed his daughter and son-in-law as senior White House advisers while they retained complex private interests
cases.status: Documented appointments; lawful under DOJ interpretation
cases.summary: Trump appointed daughter Ivanka Trump and son-in-law Jared Kushner to influential White House positions despite their limited government experience and extensive private financial interests. The Justice Department's Office of Legal Counsel concluded that the federal anti-nepotism statute did not bar presidential appointments to the White House Office. Ethics disclosures, recusals, and security-clearance concerns nevertheless followed throughout their service.
cases.significance: Legal permissibility does not resolve the democratic problem of concentrating public authority in unelected family members whose private holdings create recurring conflict questions.

cases.title: Jared Kushner's firm took billions from foreign sovereign funds after his White House service and while he returned to diplomacy
cases.status: Documented investments and fees; congressional investigation, no adjudication
cases.summary: After serving as a senior White House adviser with a major Middle East portfolio, Jared Kushner's Affinity Partners received a $2 billion commitment from Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth fund and additional foreign sovereign investment. Senate Finance investigators reported large management fees and little or no investor return through mid-2024. In 2026, congressional investigators raised new conflict concerns as Kushner again participated in U.S. Middle East diplomacy while seeking or managing foreign-government capital.
cases.significance: The sequence creates an unusually direct appearance that access and public diplomacy can be followed by private financial reward, then overlap again with official influence. The concern is structural even without proof of a quid pro quo.

sources.label: House Oversight inquiry into Saudi Arabia's $2 billion Affinity investment

cases.title: 147 Republican lawmakers voted to reject certified 2020 electoral votes after the Capitol attack
cases.status: Official congressional votes
cases.summary: After rioters attacked the Capitol, 139 House Republicans and eight Republican senators voted for at least one objection to certified electoral votes from Arizona or Pennsylvania. The votes sought to discard lawful state results even though no evidence demonstrated outcome-changing fraud.
cases.significance: Trump's election falsehood became an institutional party act. Continuing the objections after the violence normalized using Congress to undermine certified results for partisan advantage.

cases.title: Senate Republicans acquitted Trump in the Ukraine pressure case despite a separate GAO finding that the aid hold broke the law
cases.status: Senate acquittal; separate GAO legal violation finding
cases.summary: Trump was impeached for abusing presidential power by pressuring Ukraine to announce investigations useful to his reelection and for obstructing Congress. The Government Accountability Office separately concluded that the Office of Management and Budget violated the Impoundment Control Act by withholding congressionally appropriated security assistance for a policy reason. Fifty-two Republican senators voted not guilty on the abuse-of-power article; Mitt Romney was the lone Republican vote to convict.
cases.significance: The episode shows party protection operating even where a nonpartisan congressional watchdog found the underlying aid hold unlawful. The Senate vote was a constitutional and political judgment, not a finding that the hold was legal.

cases.title: Forty-three Republican senators acquitted Trump after January 6, even as McConnell called him morally responsible
cases.status: Senate acquittal despite a 57-vote majority for conviction
cases.summary: The Senate voted 57-43 to convict Trump of incitement of insurrection, ten votes short of the two-thirds threshold. All 43 not-guilty votes came from Republicans. Mitch McConnell voted to acquit on the theory that the Senate lacked jurisdiction after Trump left office, then said Trump was 'practically and morally responsible' for provoking the events and had fed supporters falsehoods.
cases.significance: The record makes the enabling mechanism unusually explicit: a party leader acknowledged grave responsibility while using a contested procedural theory to avoid the constitutional consequence.

cases.title: Trump publicly treated repentance as largely unnecessary for himself
cases.status: documented statement
cases.summary: At the Family Leadership Summit on July 18, 2015, Trump said he was not sure he had ever asked God for forgiveness and described Communion as his way of feeling cleansed. In a July 22 CNN interview he explained that he tried to live so that he did not need to ask forgiveness. In September he told CBN that asking forgiveness was important, but continued to describe Communion as asking in his own way.
cases.significance: Repentance is not an optional ornament in historic Christianity; it is the starting summons of Jesus' public ministry and a continuing practice for believers. A leader who markets himself to Christians while presenting himself as unusually exempt from confession reverses the faith's moral grammar: humility is replaced by self-certification.

sources.label: Full transcript of the July 18, 2015 Family Leadership Summit exchange
July 22 follow-up interview on forgiveness and repentance
September 2015 interview in which Trump revised and qualified his answer

cases.faithAnalysis: Jesus begins his proclamation with a call to repent and believe; repentance is central, not exceptional. Claiming to be without sin is self-deception; confession is a mark of truthfulness before God.

cases.title: The Access Hollywood recording normalized sexual entitlement as celebrity privilege
cases.status: authenticated recording
cases.summary: In a 2005 hot-mic recording published on October 7, 2016, Trump boasted in graphic terms about pursuing a married woman, kissing women without waiting, and grabbing women because celebrity let him do it. Trump apologized for the words as locker-room talk. The tape is direct evidence of his speech and claimed attitude; it is not, by itself, proof that every act described occurred.
cases.significance: The Christian problem is not merely vulgar vocabulary. The recorded posture treats women as objects and fame as permission, contradicting Jesus' teaching on lust, fidelity, and the equal dignity of neighbors. Evangelical political support that minimized the tape converted moral witness into a transactional exception for a powerful ally.

sources.label: Contemporaneous report with the recording and its provenance
Report linking to NBC's unedited video and reproducing the core statements
Trump's recorded apology and response on the day of publication

cases.faithAnalysis: Jesus intensifies the command against adultery to include lustful objectification and demands truthful speech. Christian holiness requires control of one's body and rejection of exploitative lust.

cases.title: A civil jury found Trump liable for sexually abusing and then defaming E. Jean Carroll
cases.status: civil liability affirmed; Supreme Court review denied
cases.summary: On May 9, 2023, a federal jury found Trump liable under the civil preponderance standard for sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll and defaming her, awarding $5 million. The Second Circuit affirmed the judgment on December 30, 2024. On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court denied Trump's petition for review without noted dissent, leaving the judgment intact; Trump continues to deny the assault and has pursued post-judgment litigation.
cases.significance: This is not a partisan rumor or merely an accusation: it is an adjudicated civil finding that survived appellate review. Treating such a finding as morally irrelevant because the defendant advances favored policies turns proclaimed concern for sexual morality and victims into selective ethics governed by political usefulness.

sources.label: Second Circuit opinion affirming the $5 million sexual-abuse and defamation judgment
sources.publisher: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit

sources.label: Current account of the Supreme Court's June 29, 2026 denial and the underlying verdict

cases.title: Trump was convicted of falsifying records in the election-related hush-money scheme
cases.status: felony conviction; appeal pending
cases.summary: On May 30, 2024, a New York jury convicted Trump on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records. The records disguised reimbursements to Michael Cohen, who paid Stormy Daniels before the 2016 election to suppress her account of a 2006 sexual encounter; Trump denies that encounter. Trump received an unconditional discharge on January 10, 2025, and the conviction remained in place while appellate proceedings continued as of July 13, 2026.
cases.significance: The Christian critique does not depend on proving the alleged affair, which the criminal trial did not adjudicate. It rests on a unanimous criminal verdict that business records were falsified as part of a concealment scheme, colliding with teachings against false witness, crooked dealing, and the use of wealth to hide accountability.

sources.label: New York court decision recording the jury's guilty verdict on all 34 counts

cases.title: Trump admitted misuse of charitable assets and paid $2 million under court order
cases.status: settled with admissions and court-ordered payment
cases.summary: A New York court approved the Trump Foundation's dissolution and ordered Donald Trump to pay $2 million to charities after charitable funds were used in ways tied to his political interests. The resolution included 19 admissions and restrictions on future charitable service if he creates another charity. The remaining foundation assets were distributed under judicial supervision.
cases.significance: Using a charity as an extension of personal or political power profanes the very act of giving. It is an especially direct contradiction of Christian teaching that almsgiving serves the needy without self-promotion and that stewards must not divert entrusted resources for themselves.

cases.title: Trump sustained false election claims after his own advisers and institutions rejected them
cases.status: officially documented falsehood campaign
cases.summary: The certified 2020 Electoral College result was 306 votes for Joseph Biden and 232 for Trump. The House January 6 Select Committee found that Trump's campaign officials, Justice Department leaders, advisers, and courts repeatedly told him his outcome-changing fraud claims were false or unsupported, yet he continued using them to pressure officials and the public. A later federal indictment alleged knowing deceit, but that prosecution did not produce a verdict and should not be used as proof of guilt.
cases.significance: Truth is not a dispensable tactic in Christian ethics. Repeating claims after authoritative correction, especially to retain power and mobilize followers against a lawful transfer, makes false witness a governing method. The resulting injury to neighbors, election workers, and peaceful civic order also violates the command to make peace rather than sow discord.

sources.label: Official 2020 Electoral College results and certification record
Final report of the Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
sources.publisher: U.S. Government Publishing Office / House Select Committee

sources.label: GovInfo collection of report chapters, videos, transcripts, and supporting material
Federal indictment spelling out allegations and repeated corrections Trump received

cases.title: Zero tolerance separated thousands of children without adequate reunification planning
cases.status: official inspector-general findings
cases.summary: The Trump administration's zero-tolerance approach prosecuted adults who crossed the southwest border with children, producing family separations. DOJ's inspector general found that leadership understood separation would result, underestimated legal and operational complexity, and pursued prosecutions at the expense of considering harm to children and the ability to reunite families. DOJ OIG reported more than 3,000 children separated before the practice was largely curtailed by Trump's June 20, 2018 executive order; HHS identified tracking failures and additional separated children.
cases.significance: Even when a government lawfully enforces a border, deliberately choosing a process that predictably tears children from parents without a workable reunification system is a severe moral failure. The contrast with Christian defense of family unity, care for children, mercy, and welcome of strangers is direct rather than metaphorical.

sources.label: DOJ OIG review of planning, implementation, child impact, and reunification failures
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Justice Office of Inspector General

cases.title: Trump reduced refugee protection to the lowest admissions ceiling in the program's history
cases.status: enacted presidential policy
cases.summary: Trump's January 27, 2017 order suspended the refugee program for 120 days, suspended Syrian refugee entry, and capped fiscal-year 2017 admissions at 50,000. His fiscal-year 2021 determination set a ceiling of 15,000, which the USCCB identified as the lowest since Congress created the modern refugee program in 1980 and far below the previous historical average cited by the bishops.
cases.significance: Security screening and admission limits are legitimate policy questions. The Christian grievance is the administration's sustained contraction of refuge amid global need, especially when paired with rhetoric treating displaced populations principally as threats. The result clashes with a faith whose central judgment scene includes whether the stranger was welcomed.

sources.label: January 27, 2017 refugee suspension and entry order
Presidential determination setting the fiscal-year 2021 ceiling at 15,000
USCCB historical comparison and Christian critique

cases.faithAnalysis: Welcoming the stranger and serving the vulnerable are presented as service to Christ himself. The bishops described refugee welcome as an act of Christian love and hope and called the 15,000 proposal heartbreaking.

faithLens.teaching: Welcoming the stranger and serving the vulnerable are presented as service to Christ himself.
The bishops described refugee welcome as an act of Christian love and hope and called the 15,000 proposal heartbreaking.
faithLens.reference: USCCB response to the fiscal-year 2021 refugee ceiling, October 2, 2020

cases.title: Trump used St. John's Church and a Bible as presidential imagery after a forceful park clearing
cases.status: documented event with material investigative nuance
cases.summary: On June 1, 2020, law enforcement cleared protesters from Lafayette Park; Trump then walked to St. John's Episcopal Church and posed holding a Bible without consulting the diocese. Bishop Mariann Budde said he used the church and Bible to claim spiritual authority for a message she considered contrary to Jesus. A 2021 Interior Department inspector-general review found Park Police planned the clearing for fence installation, not for Trump's visit, while also finding ineffective warnings and serious multi-agency communication failures; it did not review every use of force.
cases.significance: The most defensible criticism is not the disproven shorthand that Park Police conceived the clearing solely for a photo opportunity. It is that Trump chose to convert a church and scripture into a display of dominance at a moment demanding grief, racial justice, restraint, and pastoral presence. The church's own bishop regarded that symbolism as a misuse of Christian authority.

sources.label: Interior OIG operational review and findings
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of the Interior Office of Inspector General

sources.label: Bishop Budde's sworn congressional testimony
White House explanation of why Trump stood at St. John's

cases.faithAnalysis: Jesus warns against public religious performance used to be seen and honored by others. The Episcopal bishop responsible for St. John's said a Bible was used to claim spiritual authority over state violence and called for healing and justice.

faithLens.teaching: Jesus warns against public religious performance used to be seen and honored by others.
The Episcopal bishop responsible for St. John's said a Bible was used to claim spiritual authority over state violence and called for healing and justice.
faithLens.reference: Bishop Mariann Budde testimony to Congress, June 29, 2020

cases.title: Trump made retribution and domestic-enemy rhetoric a campaign promise
cases.status: documented campaign rhetoric
cases.summary: At CPAC on March 4, 2023, Trump told supporters he would be their retribution. In an October 13, 2024 Fox Business interview he called domestic opponents the enemy from within, named Adam Schiff, and said radical-left unrest could be handled by the National Guard or, if necessary, the military. Trump and allies framed the remarks as protection against disorder and politicized government, but the language personalized state power around vengeance and political enemies.
cases.significance: Jesus blesses peacemakers, commands love of enemies, and rejects retaliation as the disciple's governing posture. A movement that presents a leader as an instrument of believers' retribution baptizes resentment and makes cruelty to opponents feel like faithfulness.

sources.label: Full CPAC speech containing the retribution pledge
Transcript of the October 13, 2024 Fox Business interview

cases.title: The second-term mass-deportation program subordinated migrant dignity to an invasion frame
cases.status: enacted executive policy and funded program
cases.summary: On January 20, 2025, Trump ordered broad enforcement against inadmissible and removable noncitizens, expanded detention and expedited removal, enlisted state and local agencies, and revoked the family-reunification task force order. The White House called the border situation an invasion and later promoted funding for at least one million removals annually. Pope Francis answered that nations may secure borders and remove serious offenders but that identifying irregular status with criminality and indiscriminate mass deportation damages human dignity and families.
cases.significance: The Vatican response supplies a rigorous Christian standard rather than an open-borders caricature: lawful order and public safety are legitimate, but every migrant retains dignity, and force cannot become the controlling truth about a person. Trump's categorical invasion language and scale-first deportation design invite collective suspicion in place of individualized justice and mercy.

sources.label: Executive order on detention, removal, enforcement priorities, and state partnerships
White House America First priorities describing the deportation operation and refugee suspension
White House summary of legislation funding at least one million annual removals
Pope Francis' direct response to the mass-deportation program

cases.title: Trump's signature 2025 law shifted resources upward while cutting health and food support
cases.status: enacted law with nonpartisan distributional estimate
cases.summary: Congress enacted and Trump signed Public Law 119-21, promoted as the One Big Beautiful Bill. CBO's final distributional analysis found that resources generally decrease for households near the bottom of the income distribution, mainly because of reductions tied to Medicaid and SNAP, while increasing for middle- and top-income households, with the largest dollar gains at the top. The USCCB president said the law failed the poor and vulnerable despite provisions the bishops supported.
cases.significance: Budgets are moral documents because they expose who bears sacrifice and who receives advantage. Extending tax benefits while reducing food and health supports for households with the least resources reverses the Christian preferential concern for the poor; attaching religious branding to the same coalition deepens the hypocrisy rather than curing it.

sources.label: CBO final interactive distributional analysis of Public Law 119-21
CBO report on distributional effects of the House-passed H.R. 1
USCCB moral assessment after passage

cases.title: Trump ordered aggressive expansion of capital punishment and framed death as the only proper justice
cases.status: executive order in force
cases.summary: Trump's January 20, 2025 executive order declared capital punishment an essential deterrent and the only proper punishment for the vilest crimes. It directed the attorney general to pursue death sentences broadly, encourage state use, secure lethal-injection drugs, and seek reversal of Supreme Court precedents limiting capital punishment. The order also singled out capital crimes committed by people unlawfully present in the country.
cases.significance: Christian traditions differ on capital punishment, so the critique should not pretend unanimity. The contradiction is categorical for contemporary Catholic teaching, which calls the death penalty inadmissible because human dignity persists even after grave crime and redemption must remain possible. The order's language of monstrosity and ultimate retribution also leaves little room for mercy.

sources.label: Executive order restoring and expanding federal capital-punishment policy
Official Vatican text of revised Catechism paragraph 2267

cases.title: At Charlie Kirk's memorial, forgiveness met Trump's stated hatred of opponents
cases.status: documented memorial remarks; accused man charged and not convicted
cases.summary: At Charlie Kirk's September 21, 2025 memorial, Erika Kirk said she forgave the man charged with killing her husband because forgiveness was what Christ did and that hate should not answer hate. Trump later praised Charlie for not hating opponents, then said he disagreed: he hated his opponent and did not want the best for them, adding an apology to Erika. The remarks create a documented moral contrast. Trump did not explicitly call Erika weak or directly condemn her forgiveness. As of July 13, 2026, Tyler Robinson was charged, had not entered a plea, and had not been ordered to stand trial; final preliminary-hearing arguments were scheduled for September 1.
cases.significance: The contrast occurred inside an explicitly Christian memorial and requires little speculation. Erika Kirk articulated Christ-centered forgiveness in the face of devastating loss; Trump answered the theme by publicly affirming hatred and ill will toward political opponents. That posture directly opposes Jesus' commands to forgive, love enemies, and pray for persecutors, and it exposes how vengeance can be marketed as strength even when a grieving Christian chooses mercy.

sources.label: Official transcript of Trump's memorial remarks
sources.publisher: Government Publishing Office / Compilation of Presidential Documents

sources.label: Contemporaneous report documenting both Erika Kirk's forgiveness and Trump's remarks
Current report on the unresolved preliminary-hearing status
Formal charging record in State v. Tyler James Robinson

cases.title: America First made national advantage the administration's controlling moral horizon
cases.status: declared governing doctrine
cases.summary: Trump's 2017 inaugural address declared that decisions on trade, taxes, immigration, and foreign affairs would be made under an America First vision. His 2025 inaugural and day-one priorities renewed the doctrine across border policy, refugee suspension, energy, and foreign affairs. The position is a national-interest doctrine, not automatically a theological claim, but Trump repeatedly embedded it in religious language about divine favor and national restoration.
cases.significance: Christian teaching permits patriotism and recognizes governments' duties to their people. It does not permit national self-interest to become the final measure of moral action: neighbor-love crosses borders, every person bears dignity, and no nation owns the Gospel. America First becomes spiritually deranged when national victory displaces solidarity, truth, mercy, or obligations to outsiders.

sources.label: 2017 inaugural address defining America First across major policy domains
2025 inaugural address renewing America First and mass-removal commitments
Second-term America First priorities

cases.title: Trump elevated a prosperity-associated spiritual adviser despite major evangelical theological objections
cases.status: documented advisory and government appointments
cases.summary: Paula White-Cain joined Trump's 2016 evangelical advisory circle, became a White House faith adviser in his first term, and on February 7, 2025 was appointed senior adviser to the new White House Faith Office. Christianity Today describes her as Trump's longtime prayer partner and a preacher commonly associated with prosperity teaching. Trump is free to seek counsel across traditions, but his repeated elevation of White placed a disputed wealth-and-blessing theology near federal religious outreach.
cases.significance: The alliance links Trump's brand of wealth, victory, and personal destiny to a religious current many evangelicals themselves reject as spiritually harmful. The global evangelical Lausanne Movement warns that prosperity teaching can measure blessing by wealth, replace repentance with giving to a preacher, and turn faith toward self-interest rather than sacrifice.

sources.label: White House announcement appointing White-Cain senior adviser to the Faith Office
Christianity Today account of both first- and second-term appointments and evangelical controversy
Executive order establishing the 2025 White House Faith Office
Global evangelical critique of prosperity teaching

cases.title: Trump fused scripture, patriotic texts, commerce, and Christian voter mobilization
cases.status: documented commercial endorsement and campaign speech
cases.summary: During Holy Week 2024, Trump promoted a $59.99 God Bless the USA Bible containing the King James Bible alongside the Declaration of Independence, Constitution, Bill of Rights, Pledge of Allegiance, and Lee Greenwood lyrics; the seller said Trump's name and image were licensed and that campaign funds did not receive proceeds. At Turning Point's Believers' Summit on July 26, Trump explicitly mobilized Christians as a voting bloc and told them that after this election they would not have to vote again because things would be fixed.
cases.significance: This is Christian-nationalist fusion in unusually concrete form: sacred text, founding documents, patriotic song, a candidate's endorsement, and electoral mobilization packaged together. The theological objection is not to Christians voting or owning patriotic Bibles; it is to collapsing the Gospel into national identity and merchandising religious authority around a political leader.

sources.label: Official product site explaining Trump's endorsement and the Bible's contents
Contemporaneous report reproducing Trump's Holy Week promotion and licensing disclosures
Full transcript of Trump's Believers' Summit speech
Baptist-led Christian statement defining and rejecting Christian nationalism
sources.publisher: Baptist Joint Committee for Religious Liberty

cases.title: A federal task force singled out Christianity for government protection
cases.status: executive order in force
cases.summary: Trump's February 6, 2025 executive order created a cabinet-level Task Force to Eradicate Anti-Christian Bias. It directed agencies to identify and remedy alleged anti-Christian policies from the prior administration and recommend further presidential or legislative action. The order invokes real religious-liberty laws, but it establishes a faith-specific government mechanism rather than an all-faith civil-rights review.
cases.significance: Religious liberty protects Christians and must be enforced. The Christian-nationalist danger lies in treating the dominant faith as specially entitled to the machinery of state protection, allowing a favored political coalition to define which Christianity counts and recasting equal treatment as Christian privilege. Baptist religious-liberty advocates warn that privilege is not freedom.
subjects: Task Force to Eradicate Anti-Christian Bias

cases.title: Trump gave a white nationalist and Holocaust denier a seat at Mar-a-Lago
cases.status: meeting acknowledged; prior knowledge disputed
cases.summary: Trump dined at Mar-a-Lago with Ye and Nick Fuentes on November 22, 2022. Fuentes is publicly identified as a white nationalist, antisemite, and Holocaust denier. Trump said Ye unexpectedly brought guests, that he had never met Fuentes and knew nothing about him, and that the dinner was quick and uneventful; prominent Republicans including Mike Pence condemned giving Fuentes access.
cases.significance: Even accepting Trump's account that he did not know Fuentes beforehand, a former president and newly declared candidate bears responsibility for whom he hosts and for his response once extremist identity becomes clear. Christian teaching rejects racial supremacy and antisemitism; minimizing rather than unequivocally repudiating such an encounter weakens the moral boundary around both.

sources.label: Careful fact-check reconstructing Trump's public statements after the dinner
Trump statement acknowledging the dinner and denying knowledge of Fuentes
Report on Republican condemnations and Trump's acknowledgment

cases.title: Heritage publicly credited Trump with adopting nearly two-thirds of its first-year agenda
cases.status: documented policy influence claimed by Heritage and celebrated by Trump
cases.summary: On January 23, 2018, Heritage reported that the Trump administration had embraced 64 percent of 334 recommendations in its Mandate for Leadership through budgets, regulation, implementation, or active consideration. Heritage said its staff briefed administration officials and advocated the reforms. Trump then publicly celebrated Heritage's 64 percent figure, while Heritage leaders praised the administration's adoption of their agenda.
cases.significance: This is the clearest baseline against which later denials of Heritage influence should be judged. The relationship does not make every Heritage proposal immoral, but it demonstrates a tested pipeline from think-tank blueprint to Trump governance; the site's policy entries should therefore examine Heritage proposals as probable inputs, not remote commentary.

sources.label: Heritage's one-year accounting of 334 recommendations and 64 percent adoption
Heritage record of Trump and Heritage leaders celebrating the 64 percent figure
Heritage 2017 annual report repeating its influence claim

cases.title: Project 2025's Trump-era network moved from blueprint to personnel and civil-service restructuring
cases.status: documented network continuity and policy implementation
cases.summary: At Heritage's April 21, 2022 leadership conference, Trump praised the organization for laying groundwork and detailing what the movement would do after receiving a new mandate. Project 2025's Mandate for Leadership was built by a large network that included numerous former Trump officials; Rick Dearborn, a former Trump deputy chief of staff, joined its steering committee, and Russell Vought authored its Executive Office chapter. Trump reinstated his 2020 Schedule F concept as Schedule Policy/Career on January 20, 2025, and Vought was confirmed as OMB director on February 6.
cases.significance: The evidentiary case does not require claiming Trump personally wrote or adopted every Project 2025 page. It shows personnel, ideas, and governing mechanisms traveling through a shared movement network into the second administration. Schedule Policy/Career increases presidential leverage over policy-influencing civil servants, making the Christian test of power urgent: whether authority is accountable service under law or personal loyalty enforced from above.

sources.label: Video record and Heritage framing of Trump's April 2022 keynote
Transcript of Trump's April 21, 2022 Heritage keynote
Full Project 2025 Mandate listing authors' prior Trump-administration roles
Heritage announcement placing former Trump deputy chief Rick Dearborn on Project 2025's steering committee
Trump order reinstating Schedule F as Schedule Policy/Career
Senate roll call confirming Project 2025 author Russell Vought as OMB director

cases.faithAnalysis: Jesus explicitly contrasts rulers who lord power over others with leadership expressed through service. Sound governance depends on distributed counsel rather than concentrated, loyalty-driven decision making.

cases.title: Trump again withdrew the United States from the Paris climate accord and then ordered withdrawal from the underlying UN climate convention
cases.status: Paris withdrawal effective January 27, 2026; broader convention withdrawal announced
cases.summary: Trump ordered a second U.S. withdrawal from the Paris Agreement on his first day back in office, halted international climate-finance commitments, and in January 2026 ordered withdrawal from the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change itself. UNFCCC records the Paris withdrawal as effective January 27, 2026. The administration argued that the agreements unfairly burdened the U.S. economy; the UN climate chief called the later retreat from the convention economically and strategically self-defeating.
cases.significance: Climate risk, energy markets, insurance, agriculture, migration, and technology competition cross borders. An America First test asks whether surrendering a seat in the principal global forum increases U.S. control or leaves standards, markets, and collective risk management to other powers.

cases.title: Trump withdrew the United States from the World Health Organization and recalled U.S. personnel
cases.status: Withdrawal notification completed; outstanding institutional issues remained before WHO governing bodies
cases.summary: Trump ordered the United States to withdraw from WHO, pause future funding, recall U.S. personnel and contractors, and stop participation in pandemic-agreement negotiations. WHO acknowledged the withdrawal notification in January 2026 while saying unresolved issues would go to its Executive Board and World Health Assembly. The administration cited cost, independence, and COVID-response failures; WHO disputed those claims and warned that withdrawal makes both the United States and the world less safe.
cases.significance: Pathogen surveillance, data exchange, vaccine coordination, and outbreak response are collective-action systems. The test is not whether WHO is beyond criticism, but whether leaving it produces a stronger replacement or reduces American access and influence when the next cross-border health threat arrives.

sources.label: Executive order withdrawing the United States from WHO
WHO statement acknowledging the U.S. withdrawal notification and identifying unresolved issues

cases.title: Trump threatened conditional NATO protection, while the alliance later reaffirmed Article 5 and adopted a 5 percent investment target
cases.status: Documented campaign threat and completed alliance agreement; the United States remained in NATO
cases.summary: During the 2024 campaign, Trump said he would encourage Russia to do whatever it wanted to a NATO ally he considered delinquent on defense spending. At the June 2025 Hague summit, however, all allies reaffirmed the Article 5 collective-defense guarantee and committed to reach 5 percent of GDP in defined defense and security investments by 2035. NATO reported a 20 percent real increase in European and Canadian defense spending in 2025 and said every ally met the earlier 2 percent benchmark.
cases.significance: The record is mixed and therefore revealing. Burden sharing increased—a concrete result the archive should not deny—but threatening to make treaty defense contingent on a president's payment metaphor weakens the predictability on which deterrence depends. The test measures durable alliance capacity as well as headline leverage.

sources.label: Report and verbatim account of Trump's conditional-defense threat
The Hague Summit Declaration reaffirming Article 5 and adopting the 5 percent target
NATO annual-report summary of 2025 spending gains

cases.title: Trump pressed to acquire Greenland and repeatedly left coercion hanging over an allied population that rejected annexation
cases.status: Documented pressure campaign; no annexation; Trump later said he would not use force
cases.summary: Trump revived demands for U.S. control of Greenland, at points refusing to rule out coercion and arguing the territory was necessary for national security. Greenland and Denmark rejected a sale, and polling cited by the Congressional Research Service found overwhelming Greenlandic opposition to joining the United States. In January 2026 Trump said he would not use force and instead demanded immediate negotiations.
cases.significance: A security interest does not erase another people's right to self-determination or the value of treating Denmark as an ally rather than a target. An America First policy should distinguish negotiated basing and mineral access from territorial intimidation.

sources.label: CRS analysis of U.S. interest, Greenlandic opposition, coercive statements, and the later no-force statement
CRS Arctic report documenting the broader acquisition campaign and strategic context

cases.title: Trump paired 51st-state rhetoric toward Canada with tariffs that triggered retaliation and anti-American backlash
cases.status: Documented rhetoric and trade measures; Canada remained sovereign and retaliated
cases.summary: Trump repeatedly suggested Canada should become the 51st state while imposing or threatening tariffs against the United States' largest trading partner. Canada retaliated, and CRS reported a sharp decline in Canadian views of the United States and a political rupture described by Canadian leaders as the end of the old bilateral relationship. The administration defended tariffs as leverage over trade, migration, and fentanyl.
cases.significance: Canada is a treaty ally, intelligence partner, energy supplier, and deeply integrated market. The test asks whether humiliating annexation rhetoric and broad tariffs secured durable advantages worth the consumer, business, and alliance costs—or converted interdependence into hostility.

sources.label: CRS account of 51st-state rhetoric, anti-American reaction, and bilateral rupture
CRS overview of U.S.-Canada trade tensions, tariffs, and retaliation

cases.title: Trump threatened to take back the Panama Canal despite Panama's treaty-backed sovereignty
cases.status: Documented territorial rhetoric and diplomatic pressure; canal sovereignty did not change
cases.summary: Trump said the United States should regain control of the Panama Canal and repeated the claim in his inaugural address. Panama's president rejected the demand, cited the Torrijos-Carter treaties and full Panamanian sovereignty, and denied that another country controlled canal administration. A later Rubio visit produced talks on ports, migration, and China's role, but Panama did not transfer the canal.
cases.significance: Strategic concern about Chinese-linked port investment is a legitimate subject for diplomacy. Claiming an allied country's sovereign infrastructure as American property is a different proposition—one that substitutes threat and historical grievance for treaty compliance and negotiated security cooperation.

sources.label: Panamanian president's English-language response to Trump's inaugural remarks
Panama account of President Mulino's meeting with Secretary Rubio and resulting agreements
CRS overview identifying Trump's proposal to regain control of the canal

cases.title: After the Oval Office rupture, the administration paused aid and intelligence to Ukraine as the United States voted with Russia at the UN
cases.status: Documented diplomatic break and temporary pause; assistance and intelligence sharing resumed
cases.summary: On the third anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion, the United States voted with Russia against a UN General Assembly resolution that reaffirmed Ukraine's sovereignty and demanded Russian withdrawal. Days later, after Trump and Vance confronted President Zelenskyy in the Oval Office, the administration paused military assistance and intelligence sharing. CRS records that both were resumed after March 11 talks with Ukraine.
cases.significance: Negotiating an end to war is a legitimate goal. The test asks whether leverage was applied evenhandedly and whether publicly pressuring the invaded partner—while softening attribution of Russian aggression—improved U.S. bargaining power or rewarded the aggressor's preferred framing.

sources.label: UN meeting record documenting the U.S. vote against the Ukraine-backed resolution alongside Russia
CRS ceasefire analysis documenting the aid and intelligence pauses and March 11 resumption
White House defense of Trump and Vance after the Oval Office confrontation

cases.title: Trump ordered U.S. strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities without first obtaining specific congressional authorization
cases.status: Completed military strikes; constitutional authority disputed; no merits judgment cited
cases.summary: At Trump's direction, U.S. forces struck Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan during the Israel-Iran conflict. The administration relied on the president's Article II authority rather than a new authorization for use of military force. CRS reported that members of Congress questioned the legal basis and their own constitutional war-powers role. A separate CRS assessment said the exact damage and location of Iran's enriched-uranium stockpile were initially uncertain.
cases.significance: A president may face genuine threats and limited time, but 'America First' cannot mean the executive alone decides when the nation enters hostilities. The constitutional test includes Congress, defined objectives, verified results, and an exit strategy—not only the spectacle of force.

sources.label: CRS analysis of the strikes, legal authorities, and congressional war-powers questions
CRS initial assessment of the U.S. strikes and uncertainty over damage and uranium stockpiles

cases.title: The administration used lethal military strikes against suspected drug-smuggling boats without a public criminal process
cases.status: Documented lethal operations; identities, evidence, and legal authority were contested; no judicial merits ruling cited
cases.summary: U.S. forces carried out at least three lethal strikes against vessels the administration said were tied to designated trafficking organizations, reportedly killing 17 people by late September 2025. CRS noted that members of Congress and legal observers questioned the factual disclosures, designation-to-targeting theory, and domestic and international legal authority for using military force rather than interdiction and prosecution.
cases.significance: Drug trafficking is a grave public-safety problem, but an executive claim that people are cartel members cannot substitute for disclosed evidence, legal authority, and accountability when the government uses lethal force. The America First test asks what protects Americans without normalizing unreviewable killing.

sources.label: CRS overview of the September 2025 lethal vessel strikes and legal questions
CRS sanctions update describing subsequent strikes and vessel seizures

cases.title: U.S. forces captured Nicolás Maduro as Trump asserted control over Venezuela's transition and oil revenue
cases.status: Completed capture and extraction; criminal case and international-law disputes unresolved; oil revenue held as Venezuelan sovereign property under U.S. policy
cases.summary: U.S. military strikes supported the January 3 capture and extraction of Nicolás Maduro and his wife to face U.S. criminal charges. Trump said the United States would run Venezuela during a transition and officials required sanctioned oil proceeds to move through U.S.-controlled Treasury accounts. The White House says those funds remain Venezuelan sovereign property and are not U.S. assets. Congress and the UN debated whether the operation was lawful law enforcement, an unauthorized use of force, or a violation of Venezuelan sovereignty.
cases.significance: Maduro's authoritarian record and pending U.S. charges do not eliminate constitutional and international-law constraints on regime-changing force. The archive's strongest criticism is the documented assertion of control—not the inaccurate shorthand that the United States simply stole the oil.

sources.label: CRS account of the capture, strikes, transition claims, oil arrangements, and congressional questions
White House fact sheet on U.S.-held Venezuelan oil revenue and its sovereign-property status
UN Security Council meeting record on the Venezuela operation

cases.title: Under Marco Rubio, the administration dismantled USAID, terminated most programs, and cut the diplomatic workforce
cases.status: Major reorganization and program terminations completed; spending-power litigation and congressional-authority questions continued
cases.summary: Rubio became acting USAID administrator, announced cancellation of 83 percent of USAID-managed programs, transferred remaining functions to State, and oversaw a State Department reorganization. CRS reported roughly 3,000 State employees separated through reductions in force and voluntary departures by July 2025, while USAID's independent operating role was effectively eliminated. Courts separately considered whether the administration could pause or terminate congressionally funded assistance on its own timetable.
cases.significance: Foreign assistance and professional diplomacy can be wasteful or effective and deserve audit. Eliminating capacity before Congress resolves the structure and funding question risks exchanging reform for unilateral impoundment, weaker disease and famine response, and less U.S. influence where China and Russia are ready to fill the vacuum.

sources.label: CRS overview of USAID dismantlement and Rubio's 83 percent program cancellation
CRS account of the State Department reorganization and workforce reductions

cases.title: The Supreme Court held that Trump's emergency-power statute did not authorize his sweeping tariffs
cases.status: Final Supreme Court statutory holding; administration ended the affected tariffs while other tariff authorities remained
cases.summary: Trump used the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose broad tariffs. In Learning Resources v. Trump, the Supreme Court held that IEEPA does not authorize tariffs. The administration then ended the affected measures. CBO estimated the IEEPA tariffs had collected about $150 billion and said the broader 2025 trade-policy changes temporarily raised inflation and reduced investment, output, and employment, while also lowering projected federal deficits.
cases.significance: The clearest America First failure here is institutional as well as economic: the president claimed a power Congress had not granted. The fiscal benefit from tariff revenue must stay in the record, but so must the Court's rejection and CBO's estimate that Americans bore higher prices and reduced growth.
