cases.title: The administration invoked a wartime alien-enemies law for removals, but the Supreme Court required notice and judicial review
cases.status: Presidential proclamation in effect subject to litigation; Supreme Court required habeas procedure, notice, and an opportunity to seek review
cases.summary: Trump invoked the 1798 Alien Enemies Act against alleged Tren de Aragua members and directed immediate apprehension, detention, and removal. In April the Supreme Court held that challenges must proceed through habeas petitions in the district of confinement, while confirming that detainees are entitled to notice and an opportunity for judicial review. In May the Court enjoined removals of a Texas class pending further proceedings. Stephen Miller publicly celebrated the first procedural ruling as a victory for the administration.
cases.significance: Calling migration an invasion cannot erase the Fifth Amendment. The episode shows how an administration can market a venue ruling as total vindication even while every controlling opinion preserved due process and left the central statutory question unresolved.

cases.title: Rubio's State Department reorganization closed most dedicated human-rights offices and narrowed the annual reports
cases.status: Completed organizational changes; effects on report quality and U.S. leverage remain contested
cases.summary: The 2025 State Department reorganization closed most regional offices in the Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor and removed dedicated discussion of several internationally recognized rights from annual country reports. CRS recorded congressional concern about the integrity of the revised reports and the loss of specialized human-rights capacity. The administration said it was removing redundancy and aligning the department with core national interests.
cases.significance: Human-rights reporting is not charity to other countries; it supports sanctions, asylum decisions, security vetting, diplomacy, and the credibility to challenge abuses by adversaries. An America First test asks whether narrowing the evidence base makes the United States more effective or merely less willing to see inconvenient conduct by partners.

cases.title: The USFL's high-stakes antitrust strategy produced only nominal damages and no operating future
cases.status: Jury antitrust finding affirmed; $1 award trebled to $3; league ceased play
cases.summary: Trump owned the New Jersey Generals and became one of the most visible advocates for the United States Football League's move from spring football toward direct fall competition with the NFL and a massive antitrust suit. The jury found one monopolization violation but awarded only $1, automatically trebled to $3. The Second Circuit affirmed, citing evidence that the USFL's own strategy, overspending, product problems, and move to the fall caused much of its injury.
cases.significance: The episode is an early public test of Trump-style leverage: escalate, sue for an enormous sum, and expect the opponent or court to produce the business outcome. The legal win was real but commercially hollow, and the court record placed substantial responsibility on the league's own choices.

cases.title: Trump's public casino company entered Chapter 11 twice amid recurring losses and heavy debt
cases.status: Two documented corporate Chapter 11 filings; not personal bankruptcy
cases.summary: SEC filings show Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts and subsidiaries filed a prearranged Chapter 11 case in November 2004 after recurring operating losses and a large accumulated deficit. The reorganized Trump Entertainment Resorts and several subsidiaries filed Chapter 11 again in February 2009. Trump had reduced ownership and resigned from the board around the second filing; neither case was a personal bankruptcy.
cases.significance: Chapter 11 is a lawful restructuring tool, not proof of a crime. It is still a measurable counterweight to a personal brand built on unmatched deal-making: creditors and shareholders repeatedly absorbed the consequences of leverage and losses while the Trump name remained the sales asset.

sources.label: 2004 annual filing documenting recurring losses, accumulated deficit, and Chapter 11

cases.title: The Carrier announcement preserved a job quota but did not stop hundreds of promised layoffs
cases.status: Partially performed incentive agreement; 632 layoffs formally reported
cases.summary: Trump and Pence celebrated an agreement under which Carrier would maintain at least 1,069 positions at its Indianapolis facility in exchange for up to $7 million in Indiana incentives. Carrier later filed a state WARN notice for 632 layoffs as other work moved to Mexico. The White House said those layoffs had already been contemplated and that the company would still meet the retained-job quota.
cases.significance: The announcement was not wholly fictitious: jobs were retained. The Deal Test asks whether the public understood the limited quota and taxpayer incentive, or was sold the broader impression that Trump had stopped the offshoring plan. A partial rescue should be measured as partial.

sources.label: Indiana WARN database recording 632 Carrier layoffs
Report on the retained-job quota, incentives, and later layoffs

cases.title: The Foxconn Wisconsin megadeal was cut from a 13,000-job vision to a much smaller incentive contract
cases.status: Original project abandoned and contract renegotiated; smaller operations and new investment later continued
cases.summary: Trump and Wisconsin leaders promoted Foxconn's proposed $10 billion LCD campus and up to 13,000 jobs. In 2021 Wisconsin renegotiated the agreement after the original manufacturing plan did not materialize, reducing the state incentive ceiling from $2.85 billion to $80 million and the job target to 1,454. Foxconn maintained a smaller presence and in 2025 announced additional investment and jobs, which is real but far below the original vision.
cases.significance: The gap between groundbreaking spectacle and enforceable delivery is the essence of the Deal Test. Performance-based credits limited some state exposure, but local infrastructure and public planning were organized around a project that was radically downsized.

sources.label: Wisconsin announcement of the original 13,000-job Foxconn plan
Wisconsin announcement of the renegotiated contract and reduced targets
WEDC announcement of Foxconn's later additional investment and jobs

cases.title: Trump's summit diplomacy reduced immediate tension but produced no verified North Korean denuclearization plan
cases.status: Summit commitments not converted into a verified denuclearization agreement; negotiations stalled
cases.summary: The 2018 Singapore statement committed the parties to new relations, peace, remains recovery, and work toward denuclearization, while Trump unilaterally suspended major U.S.-South Korean exercises. CRS found that the statement lacked a timetable, definitions, missile limits, and verification. The 2019 Hanoi summit ended without agreement; apart from reduced tension and the return of some remains, implementation largely stalled while North Korea advanced weapons capabilities.
cases.significance: Meeting an adversary is not itself failure, and reduced nuclear-war risk was valuable. The deal claim fails where personal rapport and ceremony substituted for sequenced obligations, verification, and reciprocal concessions—the machinery that turns a summit into an enduring security result.

sources.label: CRS history and assessment of the 2018–2019 Trump-Kim negotiations
CRS overview of missing verification, stalled talks, and subsequent weapons advances

cases.title: China fell more than $217 billion short of the Phase One purchase commitments in Trump's trade deal
cases.status: Signed agreement with partial implementation and major purchase shortfall; renewed USTR enforcement review
cases.summary: Trump's Phase One agreement required specified Chinese purchases and commitments on intellectual property, technology transfer, agriculture, and financial services. USTR later found that China implemented some provisions but fell far short of the 2020–2021 purchase promises and did not address core state-led economic practices. In 2025 USTR estimated purchases were more than $217 billion below the agreement's aggregate commitments and opened a new Section 301 investigation.
cases.significance: Tariffs created leverage and the agreement produced some agricultural and market-access gains. But an enforceable deal is tested by compliance, not signature-day adjectives. The central numerical promise was missed by an extraordinary margin while the structural conflict continued.

sources.label: USTR 2022 report assessing Phase One implementation and limitations
sources.publisher: Office of the U.S. Trade Representative

sources.publisher: Office of the U.S. Trade Representative

cases.title: Trump's Doha agreement gave the Taliban a withdrawal timetable while excluding the Afghan government and weakening its forces
cases.status: Signed agreement followed by U.S. withdrawal and Afghan state collapse; responsibility spans two administrations and long-running structural failures
cases.summary: The Doha agreement committed the United States to withdraw troops and contractors in exchange for Taliban counterterrorism assurances and a promise of intra-Afghan talks. The Afghan government was not a signatory and was pressured to release thousands of Taliban prisoners. Trump drew U.S. forces down to 2,500; Biden carried out the final withdrawal; and the Taliban's ensuing advance culminated in the Afghan government's collapse amid long-running dependency, corruption, political division, and security-force weakness.
cases.significance: Ending a twenty-year war was a legitimate and widely shared objective. The Deal Test asks whether the terms protected the partner government, sequenced performance, and preserved leverage. Here the United States granted the Taliban's core demand up front while the promised political settlement never became a durable barrier to takeover.

sources.label: Afghanistan: Background and U.S. Policy in Brief
U.S. Military Withdrawal and Taliban Takeover in Afghanistan: Frequently Asked Questions

cases.title: The Pentagon accepted Qatar's luxury 747 for presidential use and spent hundreds of millions retrofitting it
cases.status: Foreign-government gift accepted and aircraft placed in service; constitutional, ethics, cost, and security objections not adjudicated as bribery
cases.summary: The Defense Department accepted a Boeing 747-8 from Qatar for use as a presidential aircraft. By July 2026 the modified jet had entered service after a reported $400 million retrofit, but Trump switched to an older aircraft during a return trip from the NATO summit amid renewed security questions about the new plane's defensive systems. Members of Congress introduced measures asserting an Emoluments Clause problem and seeking to block spending, but those political claims are not a judicial bribery finding.
cases.significance: A gift can save acquisition cost and still create dependency, retrofit expense, security compromise, and the appearance that access to a president is a market. The proper claim is a documented foreign-gift conflict—not the legally unsupported shorthand 'bribe plane.'

sources.label: Report on Pentagon acceptance of the Qatari aircraft
Current report on retrofit cost, operational use, and security questions
Congressional Record text of a proposed Senate resolution objecting to the transfer

cases.title: Trump's stop-start tariff campaign raised prices and uncertainty before the Supreme Court removed its emergency-law foundation
cases.status: CBO macroeconomic estimates and final Supreme Court statutory ruling; other tariffs and trade negotiations continued
cases.summary: The administration repeatedly announced, paused, modified, and negotiated broad tariffs while claiming they would restore industry and force better agreements. CBO estimated the 2025 tariff changes temporarily increased inflation and reduced investment, output, and employment, while also generating revenue and lowering federal borrowing. In February 2026 the Supreme Court held that IEEPA did not authorize the emergency tariffs, forcing the administration to end that subset while leaving other statutory tariffs in place.
cases.significance: Tariffs can be lawful industrial policy; volatility is not a strategy by itself. A deal must be scored against consumer prices, retaliation, business planning, enforceable concessions, revenue, and final legal authority—not the volume of announcement-day claims.

sources.label: CBO estimate of tariff effects on inflation, output, investment, employment, and deficits
Supreme Court opinion holding IEEPA does not authorize tariffs
sources.publisher: Supreme Court of the United States

sources.label: CBO update after termination of the IEEPA tariffs

cases.title: After abandoning the Iran nuclear deal, Trump reached war and a de facto Hormuz closure without a durable replacement accord
cases.status: JCPOA withdrawal completed; subsequent nuclear escalation, U.S. combat, oil disruption, and fragile 2026 arrangement documented; causation is multi-factor
cases.summary: Trump withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018 without securing a replacement nuclear agreement. Iran later expanded enrichment well beyond JCPOA limits amid sanctions, regional conflict, Israeli attacks, U.S. strikes in 2025, and direct U.S.-Iran combat in 2026. The Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed for more than three months, shutting in millions of barrels per day and driving oil prices higher. A June 2026 memorandum reduced fighting, but the UN called it fragile as confrontations resumed in July.
cases.significance: The comparison is to Trump's own promised alternative: a stronger, safer deal. Eight years after exit, the measurable position was a larger Iranian nuclear program, a direct war, disrupted shipping, and a provisional arrangement rather than the comprehensive replacement that maximum pressure was supposed to deliver.

sources.label: CRS overview of Iran's nuclear program after the JCPOA and 2025 attacks
EIA assessment of Hormuz disruption, shut-in production, and oil-price effects
UN call for restraint describing the June memorandum as fragile amid renewed confrontation
sources.publisher: United Nations Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs

sources.label: White House account of the June 2026 Iran memorandum
